text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Yesterday we asked a question that we must now answer. We saw the Table of Showbread; its three names, its acacia wood and gold construction, its crown of gold, its vessels of pure gold, and its position at the same height as the Ark on the north wall of the Holy Place. But we left at the table itself and had not yet reached what was on top of it. Today we open the bread, look at every detail of what the showbread was and how it was made, meet the remarkable family who baked it, and then close with a picture of what all of this means for the congregation that carries this table on their shoulders today.

The instructions for the bread are given in Leviticus 24:5-9. The bread was made from the finest grade of wheat flour; not ordinary flour but the most refined and pure available, sifted many times over to remove every lump, every impurity, every rough grain until what remained was smooth, clean, and without any coarseness. Only this flour was acceptable. God did not allow average flour on His table. The finest was required because the table stood before the finest; before the face of the King of all creation. Each of the twelve loaves required two-tenths of an ephah of this fine flour, approximately six pounds of flour per loaf. These were not thin wafers or small symbolic pieces. They were substantial, generous loaves; real bread, with real weight, baked to a full size.

Every loaf was exactly the same size as every other. No tribe received a bigger loaf than another. No tribe received a smaller one. Before the face of Yahweh, all twelve tribes stood equal, all equally fed, all equally represented. The twelve loaves were arranged on the table in two rows of six; two stacks, one on the left side of the table and one on the right, each stack rising six loaves high. Always in order. Always complete. Always twelve. And on top of each stack sat a golden cup filled with pure frankincense, its fragrance rising quietly in the holy room, representing the prayers and worship of the people ascending before God.

Every Sabbath without fail, the old loaves were replaced with freshly baked ones. The priests carried in the new bread as the old bread was removed. Not a single Sabbath passed without this renewal. And when the old loaves were removed from the table, they were not discarded or treated as common food. They were eaten by Aaron and his sons inside the Holy Place itself; not taken outside,

not eaten at home, not distributed to the camp. They had to be consumed in the holy room, in the presence of God. This bread was described as most holy. It could not be handled as an ordinary thing even after it had left the table.

Here is a detail that is easy to pass over but should not be. First Chronicles 9:32 and 23:29 tell us that the Kohathites were responsible not only for carrying the Table of Showbread through the wilderness but also for baking the showbread and setting it in order each week. The same people who carried the table also prepared what sat on it. Their responsibility was not simply to lift and walk. It ran from the flour to the fire to the carrying to the placing before the face of God. Every part of the table’s life was in Kohathite hands.

Among the Kohathites there was a particular family mentioned in the Jerusalem & Babylonian Talmud known as the Garmu, we also read about this in 1 Chronicles 9:32 as "Some of their kinsmen of the Kohathites had charge of the showbread, to prepare it every Sabbath." They held the secret knowledge of how to bake the showbread correctly. The preparation of this bread required a specific skill that was not widely known, and the Garmu family guarded it carefully.
When people once asked them to teach others the method, they refused. Their reason was not pride or selfishness. It was reverence. They said: “We know that the temple will one day be destroyed, and we will not allow an unworthy man to learn how to bake this bread and then use that knowledge to serve an idol.”
They would rather hold the secret alone, even at personal cost, than see the sacred bread of the presence replicated in the service of a false god. They guarded the holiness of the bread with their lives.

This zeal in the Garmu family is a picture of something that should be in every person who carries the things of God. They did not treat the showbread as ordinary baking. They treated it as a sacred trust. What was placed before the face of God was not to be reproduced cheaply or misused carelessly. The Kohathites carried the table and they prepared the bread on it, and they kept both with the deepest reverence, from the beginning of the work to the end. This is the spirit that should mark the congregation today when we carry what belongs to God.

Now we can stand back and see the whole picture. The Table of Showbread was a gold table spread by God in the middle of the wilderness. On it sat twelve loaves of the finest bread, arranged in perfect order, representing every one of His people before His face. It was never empty. It was never forgotten. It was renewed every Sabbath. It was carried through the desert wandering on Kohathite shoulders.
And it was always in the presence of Yahweh, standing at the same height as the Ark, bread and glory side by side.

When we carry this table on our shoulders as the new covenant congregation, we are carrying the testimony that God feeds His people. Not occasionally. Not when circumstances are favourable. Continuously. The table never went empty through the entire wilderness. No matter how dry the desert was around it, no matter how long the journey felt, no matter how far the Promised Land seemed; the bread was always there. We carry that testimony into the world. We carry the news that God’s provision does not run dry, that His people are never absent from His face, and that every single one of them; all twelve, not ten, not eleven, is remembered and represented before the King.

We also carry a personal responsibility. The Kohathites baked the bread before they carried the table. They could not carry something they had not first prepared. In the same way, we cannot carry to others what we have not first received.
We cannot feed a starving world with a bread we have never eaten ourselves.
The congregation that carries the Table of Showbread must first sit at that table. First feed on the Word. First receive the presence. First be nourished before carrying the nourishment. The Garmu family baked the bread with their hands before it was ever set before God. The new creation Kohathite must feed on Christ personally before they can carry the testimony of His provision to anyone else.

title: Prayer
text:
Yahweh, You are my shepherd and I shall not want (Psalm 23:1). You spread a table before Your people in the wilderness and You never let it go empty for a single day. Not one Sabbath passed without fresh bread. Not one week went without the frankincense rising before Your face on behalf of Your people.

We thank You that You are the God who provides, who remembers, who keeps all twelve loaves on the table and leaves none out. Forgive us for the times we have tried to carry the table without first sitting at it; for the times we have tried to feed others with a bread we had not eaten, to give a nourishment we had not received. Teach us to feed on You first, every morning, before we carry You anywhere. And then send us out with the Table of Showbread on our shoulders; carrying the testimony that Your provision is real, that Your people are before Your face, and that Your table is never bare. May the world see through us that there is bread in the presence of God, and that it is enough.

In Christ’s name, amen.
